finding_summary: Best in-domain F1=0.320 (CNN1D/LSTM at default); ROC-AUC=0.997 for CNN1D; threshold calibration gap is the bottleneck not model quality
deployment_implication: High imbalance (40% attack); val-tuned threshold recommended for deployment

finding_summary: Best in-domain F1=0.468 (LSTM); family-disjoint split makes in-domain harder than cross-domain from diverse sources
deployment_implication: Family-disjoint evaluation; in-domain F1 is structurally low — not a model failure

finding_summary: Best in-domain F1=0.830 (RF); full category overlap and low imbalance (5.7%) make UNSW easiest in-domain target
deployment_implication: RF is best in-domain but brittle cross-domain; do not deploy RF for cross-domain use cases

finding_summary: LR F1=0.892 [0.886 0.897]; strongest single cross-domain result; linear boundary on rate/volume features generalizes to IoT C&C
deployment_implication: CIC-trained LR is viable for IoT botnet detection without target-domain adaptation

finding_summary: LR F1=0.860 [0.853 0.866]; second strongest result; UNSW diverse training transfers to IoT C&C
deployment_implication: UNSW-trained LR is also viable for IoT deployment; LR preferred over LSTM (LSTM is seed-unstable)

finding_summary: LR F1=0.731 [0.730 0.733]; only partial success on hard CIC target; LSTM F1=0.631
deployment_implication: CIC is a hard target; UNSW→CIC LR is the best option but ROC-AUC (0.643) is modest

finding_summary: Near-total failure: LR F1=0.143 is best; UNSW is a hard target from CIC
deployment_implication: Do not deploy CIC-trained models on UNSW-like environments

finding_summary: Near-total failure: LSTM F1=0.139 is best; IoT source systematically poor; all methods fail
deployment_implication: IoT-trained models cannot detect UNSW protocol-based attacks

finding_summary: Near-total failure: LSTM F1=0.261 (high seed variance); LR F1=0.086; IoT source systematically poor
confidence_tier: Tier 3 — caution required for LSTM; Tier 1 for failure conclusion
deployment_implication: IoT→CIC failure conclusion is stable; LSTM point estimate is not reliable

finding_summary: fixed_fpr degenerates (threshold→1.0) on 25/45 cross-domain rows (56%); predicts all-benign on majority of cross-domain settings
deployment_implication: NEVER use source-tuned fixed_fpr threshold for cross-domain deployment

finding_summary: val_tuned degenerates on 6/45 rows (13%); far fewer than fixed_fpr but still affects CIC LR and IoT→CIC XGB
deployment_implication: Val-tuned is safer than fixed_fpr but still requires target-domain validation

finding_summary: Default threshold (0.5) never degenerates (0/45 degenerate) but produces wildly variable performance across directions
deployment_implication: Default is the most robust threshold for cross-domain deployment; tune on target if possible

finding_summary: Models retain rank-ordering ability (ROC-AUC > 0.6) even when F1=0 at default threshold; the bottleneck is score distribution shift, not discriminative ability
deployment_implication: Implication: calibration transfer (Platt scaling) may unlock latent performance; ROC-AUC is a better measure of model quality than F1 for cross-domain comparison

finding_summary: UNSW→IoT is the only direction where fixed_fpr produces non-degenerate results; LR val_tuned F1=0.864 and fixed_fpr F1>0
deployment_implication: UNSW→IoT is unusually robust to threshold choice — LR can be deployed with any tier

finding_summary: Methods primarily substitute not complement; when they succeed they detect the same flows; when they fail they fail on the same attacks
deployment_implication: Simple OR/AND fusion does not reliably improve over best single method in cross-domain settings

finding_summary: CIC→IoT fixed_fpr OR fusion (LR+LSTM) achieves modest gain F1=0.848 vs LR alone; FPR=1.4%
deployment_implication: This is the only cross-domain setting with a deployment-worthy fusion; verify on real traffic

finding_summary: OR fusion on failed directions (IoT→UNSW, IoT→CIC, CIC→UNSW) catastrophically inflates FPR without recovering TP
deployment_implication: Never use OR fusion on cross-domain failed directions

finding_summary: UNSW-trained LR default produces 37% FPR on external benign traffic (hw1/part2.pcap 2956 flows); deployment alarm rate ~6564/hour
deployment_implication: Do not deploy UNSW-trained LR on networks with traffic profiles dissimilar to UNSW training environment

finding_summary: CIC-trained CNN1D and LSTM produce near-zero FPR on same external benign set; IoT-trained models also low FPR
deployment_implication: CIC-trained DL models are more conservative on external benign traffic; preferred for low-FPR deployment

finding_summary: False alarm rate on external benign traffic is source-dependent; same external traffic produces 37% FPR (UNSW-LR) vs 0% FPR (CIC-LSTM)
deployment_implication: Match source training environment to deployment target environment; 'high in-domain performance' does not guarantee low false alarms on external benign traffic

finding_summary: Rank signal preserved (ROC-AUC >0.6) but threshold fails: CIC→IoT XGB/LSTM, IoT→CIC LR/XGB — score distributions shift so positive-class scores fall below 0.5
deployment_implication: These models are rescueable via threshold calibration; val-tuned threshold should be tried first

finding_summary: Rank signal collapses (ROC-AUC near random): IoT→UNSW all methods, UNSW→CIC CNN1D/XGB — feature distributions qualitatively different; model learns source-specific patterns only
deployment_implication: These models cannot be rescued by threshold adjustment; need source-domain retraining or domain adaptation

finding_summary: IoT→UNSW timing features (delta_t KS=0.558): Mirai repetitive C&C timing patterns qualitatively different from UNSW protocol attacks; narrow source boundary fails to generalize
deployment_implication: Feature drift in temporal features (delta_t) is the primary degradation driver for IoT source; rich timing features would not help — they would overfit Mirai patterns more

finding_summary: IoT+UNSW→CIC: LR val_tuned F1=0.803 (+0.062 vs single-source), XGB val_tuned +0.083 — two-source diversity helps on hard target when neither source dominates
deployment_implication: Multisource is recommended for CIC-target deployment when both IoT and UNSW data are available

finding_summary: CIC+IoT→UNSW: LR val_tuned +0.032 F1 — statistically significant but operationally small; UNSW is still a hard target
deployment_implication: Marginal benefit does not justify multisource overhead for UNSW-target deployment

finding_summary: CIC+UNSW→IoT and CIC+IoT→UNSW: CIC dataset (1M+ flows) dominates training; combined LR ≈ CIC single-source; XGB collapses due to scale_pos_weight conflict
deployment_implication: Dataset size imbalance in multisource training is a critical practical challenge; subsampling or dataset weighting needed

finding_summary: CIC+UNSW→IoT LSTM fixed_fpr: +0.055 F1 [0.041 0.068] — certified but narrow; absolute F1=0.129 vs 0.078
deployment_implication: Narrow gain — real but operationally marginal; requires target-domain FPR calibration which is not available in strict cross-domain protocol

finding_summary: CIC→IoT LR, UNSW→IoT LR/XGB, all failure directions (IoT→UNSW LR/XGB), all in-domain vs cross-domain comparisons, RF brittleness, CIC in-domain DL stability: all certified
deployment_implication: These conclusions can be stated directly without additional caveats

finding_summary: UNSW→CIC LR/LSTM, Phase 5 gains, threshold tier differences (significant but often small), external benign FPR: real findings with noted limitations
deployment_implication: State with CI or effect size; contextualise limitations

finding_summary: UNSW→IoT LSTM (seed std=0.184), IoT→CIC LSTM (seed std=0.149), UNSW→CIC CNN1D (std=0.042): canonical point estimates are not reliable; may not reproduce
deployment_implication: These results MUST be presented with explicit seed variance warning; the failure conclusion for IoT→CIC remains Tier 1 — only the LSTM point estimate is uncertain
